feedback.text: I would like to express my support for the Stop Killing Games initiative and urge lawmakers to consider it as a relevant and worthy cause that aligns with the core goals of the Digital Fairness Act. The issue at hand companies remotely disabling access to legally purchased digital games is not only anti-consumer, but also undermines digital ownership and trust in digital commerce. The Digital Fairness Act is well-positioned to address this kind of unfair practice. Moreover, this concern extends beyond games. Many digital apps and services, sold with a promise of lifetime access for a one-time fee, are now being retroactively altered into subscription-based models, even for users who purchased them outright. A clear example is CamScanner, an app that I like many others paid for in full with the understanding of lifetime use. Recently, access to features previously included in the purchase has been restricted unless I subscribe again. This effectively invalidates the original purchase contract and erodes consumer confidence. I respectfully request that the scope of the Digital Fairness Act explicitly include protections for consumers of digital products and services, whether games or utility apps, by ensuring that companies honor the terms under which their products were sold. Retroactively altering access terms or rendering products unusable should be clearly defined as unfair and deceptive business practices. Thank you for considering this important issue in the effort to build a more just and trustworthy digital marketplace. Sincerely, Tibor András Gőcze Hungary 1114 Budapest Kosztolányi Dezső tér 5.
